    14‚Üí$\sqrt{m_s}/\sqrt{m_c}=2-\sqrt{3}$ is confirmed to $1.2\%$.
    15‚ÜíThe vacuum-charge doubling relation $\sqrt{m_b}=3\sqrt{m_s}+\sqrt{m_c}$ predicts
    16‚Üí$m_b=4177$ MeV ($0.1\sigma$ from PDG).
    17‚ÜíThe Koide phase parameter satisfies
    18‚Üí$\delta_0\bmod 2\pi/3 \approx 2/9$ to 35 ppm ($3.1\sigma$ with look-elsewhere, $4.3\sigma$ specific).
    19‚ÜíA meson triple $(-\pi,D_s,B)$ extends $Q\approx 2/3$ to composite hadrons at $0.1\%$.
    20‚ÜíThe dual Koide $Q(1/m_d,1/m_s,1/m_b)=0.665$ ($0.22\%$ from $2/3$) suggests a
    21‚ÜíSeiberg-dual structure.
    22‚ÜíThe de Vries angle $R=(\sqrt{19}-3)(\sqrt{19}-\sqrt{3})/16=0.22310\ldots$ matches
    23‚Üíthe on-shell $\sin^2\theta_W$ to $0.13\sigma$,
    24‚Üíand the Gatto--Sartori--Tonin relation $\sin\theta_C=\sqrt{m_d/m_s}$ holds to $0.9\%$.
    25‚ÜíTaken individually, several of these relations have modest significance; taken together,
    26‚Üítheir interconnections---shared algebraic structure, overlapping mass triplets, and
    27‚Üícommon Koide phase---point to a single organizing principle.
    28‚ÜíThe companion paper [I] constructs the $\mathcal{N}=1$ Lagrangian that realizes this
    29‚Üíprinciple via an O'Raifeartaigh superpotential with structural invariant $Q=2/3$.
    30‚Üí
    31‚Üí---
    32‚Üí
    33‚Üí## 1. Introduction
    34‚Üí
    35‚Üí### 1.1 The Koide formula
    36‚Üí
    37‚ÜíIn 1981, Koide derived a relation among the charged lepton masses from a composite model
    38‚Üíwith $S_3$ permutation symmetry acting on preon constituents [1].  Stripped of its
    39‚Üímodel-dependent origin, the relation states that the ratio
    40‚Üí$$
    41‚ÜíQ(m_1,m_2,m_3) \;\equiv\; \frac{m_1+m_2+m_3}{\bigl(\sqrt{m_1}+\sqrt{m_2}+\sqrt{m_3}\bigr)^2}
    42‚Üí$$
    43‚Üíequals $2/3$ when evaluated on the electron, muon, and tau masses.  With current PDG
    44‚Üívalues [2],
    45‚Üí$$
    46‚ÜíQ(m_e,\,m_\mu,\,m_\tau) \;=\; 0.666661 \pm 0.000007\,,
    47‚Üí$$
    48‚Üíwhere the uncertainty is dominated by $\delta m_\tau = 0.12$ MeV.  If the formula is
    49‚Üíexact, it predicts $m_\tau = 1776.97$ MeV, consistent with the experimental value
    50‚Üí$1776.86 \pm 0.12$ MeV to $0.91\sigma$.
    51‚Üí
    52‚ÜíFoot reformulated this as a geometric condition: the vector of square roots of masses
    53‚Üímakes a $45^\circ$ angle with the democratic vector $(1,1,1)$ [3].  Equivalently, in the
    54‚Üíangular parametrization
    55‚Üí$$
    56‚Üí\sqrt{m_k} \;=\; \sqrt{M_0}\,\Bigl(1 + \sqrt{2}\,\cos\!\Bigl(\tfrac{2\pi k}{3}+\delta_0\Bigr)\Bigr)\,,
    57‚Üí\qquad k=0,1,2\,,
    58‚Üí$$
    59‚Üíthe condition $Q=2/3$ is satisfied identically for any $M_0$ and $\delta_0$, and the
    60‚Üíthree masses are determined by two parameters [4].  The phase for charged leptons is
    61‚Üí$\delta_0 = 0.2222$ (in natural units of $2\pi/3$).
    62‚Üí
    63‚ÜíThe precision of the lepton relation---better than one part in $10^5$---has prompted
    64‚Üífour decades of theoretical attempts to explain it, ranging from discrete symmetries
    65‚Üí($S_3$, $A_4$) acting on flavon fields [5,6] to radiative mechanisms that protect
    66‚Üíthe formula against renormalization group running [7].  Yet no consensus model exists.
    67‚ÜíThe formula has been called an "unexplained coincidence" [8] and a "tantalizing hint"
    68‚Üí[9] in roughly equal measure.
    69‚Üí
    70‚Üí### 1.2 The skeptic's objection: look-elsewhere and scheme dependence
    71‚Üí
    72‚ÜíTwo objections are standard.
    73‚Üí
    74‚Üí**Look-elsewhere.**  With six quarks and three charged leptons forming
    75‚Üí$\binom{9}{3} = 84$ triples (or more, allowing signed square roots), a near-hit at
    76‚Üí$Q=2/3$ somewhere in the data is not implausible by chance.  We address this head-on.
    77‚ÜíA Monte Carlo simulation drawing nine masses log-uniformly over the range
    78‚Üí$[0.1,\,200{,}000]$ MeV and scanning all 84 triples times 8 sign assignments finds that
    79‚Üía match as precise as the charged leptons occurs in only $0.24\%$ of random mass
    80‚Üíspectra.  This is a look-elsewhere corrected significance of $2.8\sigma$ [10].  Not
    81‚Üíoverwhelming---but the lepton Koide is only the first entry in a longer list.
    82‚Üí
    83‚Üí**Renormalization scheme.**  The Koide formula is stated in terms of pole masses (or
    84‚Üí$\overline{\text{MS}}$ masses evaluated at the mass itself, $m_q(m_q)$).  Running to the
    85‚ÜíGUT scale shifts $Q$ by a few percent for quarks and by less than $0.1\%$ for leptons
    86‚Üí[7,11].  This is sometimes used to argue that the relation is an accident of the
    87‚Üíinfrared.  But it is equally true that many well-established mass relations---the
    88‚ÜíGell-Mann--Okubo formula, the Gatto--Sartori--Tonin (GST) relation---are statements about
    89‚Üílow-energy masses, not running couplings at a unification scale.  If the Koide relation
    90‚Üíoriginates from boundary conditions on an effective superpotential (as argued in [I]),
    91‚Üíthen the infrared is exactly where it should hold.
    92‚Üí
    93‚Üí### 1.3 Beyond the leptons: a web of mass relations
    94‚Üí
    95‚ÜíThe real case for the Koide formula rests not on a single relation but on a web of
    96‚Üíinterlocking observations.  In 2011, Rodejohann and Zhang [12] noted that the triple
    97‚Üí$(m_c, m_b, m_t)$ satisfies $Q = 0.6693$, within $0.4\%$ of $2/3$.  This was surprising
    98‚Üíbecause it mixes quark generations and charge sectors.  In [13], one of us showed that
    99‚Üíallowing signed square roots reveals a further triple $(-m_s, m_c, m_b)$ with $Q = 0.675$
   100‚Üí($1.2\%$ from $2/3$), and that these triples chain together: the output of one is the
   101‚Üíinput of the next, covering five of the six quark masses.
   102‚Üí
   103‚ÜíThe present paper collects all known mass relations of this type---ten observations
   104‚Üíspanning charged leptons, quarks, and pseudoscalar mesons---and evaluates each one
   105‚Üístatistically.  The observations, in order of discovery, are:
   106‚Üí
   107‚Üí1. **The charged-lepton Koide** [1]:
   108‚Üí   $Q(m_e,m_\mu,m_\tau) = 2/3$ to $0.001\%$.  Significance: $0.91\sigma$ in $m_\tau$;
   109‚Üí   $2.8\sigma$ after look-elsewhere.
   110‚Üí
   111‚Üí2. **The quark seed** [13]:
   112‚Üí   $Q(0,\,m_s,\,m_c) \approx 2/3$, meaning $\sqrt{m_s}/\sqrt{m_c} \approx 2-\sqrt{3}$.
   113‚Üí   Observed ratio $0.271$, theoretical $0.268$; deviation $1.2\%$.
   114‚Üí
   115‚Üí3. **The signed quark triple** [13]:
   116‚Üí   $Q(-m_s,\,m_c,\,m_b) = 0.675$, deviation $1.24\%$ from $2/3$.
   117‚Üí
   118‚Üí4. **The heavy triple** [12]:
   119‚Üí   $Q(m_c,\,m_b,\,m_t) = 0.6693$, deviation $0.40\%$.
   120‚Üí
   121‚Üí5. **The meson triple** [14]:
   122‚Üí   $Q(-m_\pi,\,m_{D_s},\,m_B) = 0.6674$, deviation $0.10\%$.
   123‚Üí
   124‚Üí6. **$v_0$-doubling** [this work]:
   125‚Üí   $\sqrt{m_b} = 3\sqrt{m_s} + \sqrt{m_c}$ to $0.07\%$, predicting $m_b = 4177$ MeV
   126‚Üí   (PDG: $4180 \pm 30$ MeV, $0.1\sigma$).  In isolation, the look-elsewhere probability
   127‚Üí   is $24.9\%$ (not significant); the significance comes from its structural role within
   128‚Üí   the triple framework.
   129‚Üí
   130‚Üí7. **The phase relation** [15,16]:
   131‚Üí   $\delta_0 \bmod 2\pi/3 \approx 2/9$ to 35 ppm.  Significance: $3.1\sigma$ with
   132‚Üí   look-elsewhere over 128 simple fractions; $4.3\sigma$ for the specific value $2/9$.
   133‚Üí
   134‚Üí8. **The dual Koide** [this work]:
   135‚Üí   $Q(1/m_d,\,1/m_s,\,1/m_b) = 0.665$, $0.22\%$ from $2/3$.
   136‚Üí   Suggests a Seiberg-dual seesaw structure $M_j \propto 1/m_j$.
   137‚Üí
   138‚Üí9. **The cyclic echo** [this work]:
   139‚Üí   Iterating the Koide equation as $t \to c \to s \to \text{stall} \to c$ produces an
   140‚Üí   algebraically exact cycle (agreement to machine epsilon).
   141‚Üí
   142‚Üí10. **The second scaling fails** [this work]:
   143‚Üí    $M_2 = 9M_0$, $\delta_2 = 9\delta_0$ does *not* produce $(c,b,t)$ or any physical
   144‚Üí    triple.  The pattern stops at one step.  This negative result constrains the
   145‚Üí    mechanism.
   146‚Üí
   147‚ÜíTwo additional relations, while not Koide triples, belong to the same family:
   148‚Üí
   149‚Üí- **The de Vries angle** [17]:
   150‚Üí  $R = (\sqrt{19}-3)(\sqrt{19}-\sqrt{3})/16 = 0.22310\ldots$ matches
   151‚Üí  $\sin^2\theta_W(\text{on-shell})$ to $0.13\sigma$ in $M_W$.
   152‚Üí
   153‚Üí- **The GST/Oakes relation** [18,19]:
   154‚Üí  $\sin\theta_C = \sqrt{m_d/m_s} = 0.224$, matching $|V_{us}| = 0.2243$ to $0.9\%$.
   155‚Üí
   156‚Üí### 1.4 What this paper adds
   157‚Üí
   158‚ÜíPrevious discussions of these relations appear scattered across individual papers,
   159‚Üíconference proceedings, and blog posts.  No systematic statistical assessment exists.
   160‚ÜíThis paper provides three things:
   161‚Üí
   162‚Üí**First**, a self-contained compilation of all the observations, with current PDG values
   163‚Üíand error bars, in a single reference.  We state every number with its uncertainty and
   164‚Üípull, not just a percentage deviation.
   165‚Üí
   166‚Üí**Second**, a rigorous look-elsewhere analysis.  For each observation we define the trial
   167‚Üíspace---how many similar relations could have been examined---and compute corrected
   168‚Üí$p$-values.  We are explicit about which observations are significant in isolation
   169‚Üí($2.8\sigma$ for the lepton Koide, $3.1\sigma$ for the phase relation) and which derive
   170‚Üítheir significance only from the structural context ($v_0$-doubling, dual Koide).
   171‚Üí
   172‚Üí**Third**, an argument that the observations are not independent.  The quark seed, the
   173‚Üísigned triple, the heavy triple, and the $v_0$-doubling relation share three of the same
   174‚Üímasses ($m_s$, $m_c$, $m_b$) and are linked by a common algebraic structure.
   175‚ÜíAn exhaustive scan of all $\binom{6}{3} = 20$ quark triples reveals that *every* triple
   176‚Üíclose to $Q = 2/3$ obligatorily mixes up-type and down-type quarks [13]; pure
   177‚Üísame-charge triples $(d,s,b)$ and $(u,c,t)$ give $Q = 0.73$ and $Q = 0.85$
   178‚Üírespectively, both far from $2/3$.  This is not a selection effect: while $90\%$ of
   179‚Üírandom triples from six quarks are mixed by combinatorics, the $100\%$ rate among
   180‚ÜíKoide-satisfying triples is a structural feature pointing toward CKM-type mixing.
   181‚Üí
   182‚ÜíThe companion paper [I] constructs the $\mathcal{N}=1$ supersymmetric Lagrangian that
   183‚Üíproduces these relations.  The key result there is that the O'Raifeartaigh superpotential
   184‚Üí$$
   185‚ÜíW = f\,\Phi_0 + m\,\Phi_a\Phi_b + g\,\Phi_0\Phi_a^2
   186‚Üí$$
   187‚Üíproduces a Koide seed triple $(0,\,m_-,\,m_+)$ with $Q = 2/3$ exactly when
   188‚Üí$gv/m = \sqrt{3}$, where $v = \langle\Phi_0\rangle$ is the pseudo-modulus vacuum
   189‚Üíexpectation value.  The mass hierarchy $m_s/m_c = (2-\sqrt{3})^2 \approx 0.072$ emerges
   190‚Üífrom the *minimal* model without additional dynamics: it is the unique mass ratio
   191‚Üíconsistent with a massless goldstino (the seed zero), the Koide condition, and the single
   192‚Üíalgebraic constraint on the superpotential couplings.
   193‚Üí
   194‚ÜíThe present paper is deliberately agnostic about the Lagrangian.  We state the empirical
   195‚Üírelations, assess their statistical weight, and leave the dynamical interpretation to [I].
   196‚ÜíThe question this paper asks is precise: *given the data, is there evidence for a common
   197‚Üíalgebraic structure in the fermion mass spectrum, or can the observations be dismissed as
   198‚Üílook-elsewhere artifacts?*
   199‚Üí
   200‚Üí### 1.5 Conventions and mass inputs
   201‚Üí
   202‚ÜíThroughout this paper, quark masses refer to $\overline{\text{MS}}$ values at the
   203‚Üíself-energy scale $m_q(m_q)$ for $c$ and $b$, and the pole mass for the top quark:
   204‚Üí$m_s = 93.4 \pm 5.8$ MeV, $m_c = 1270 \pm 20$ MeV, $m_b = 4180 \pm 30$ MeV,
   205‚Üí$m_t = 172.76 \pm 0.30$ GeV [2].  Light quark masses are $m_u = 2.16 \pm 0.49$ MeV,
   206‚Üí$m_d = 4.67 \pm 0.48$ MeV.  Lepton masses are pole masses: $m_e = 0.51100$ MeV,
   207‚Üí$m_\mu = 105.658$ MeV, $m_\tau = 1776.86 \pm 0.12$ MeV.  Meson masses are PDG
   208‚Üíaverages: $m_\pi = 139.57$ MeV, $m_{D_s} = 1968.35$ MeV, $m_B = 5279.34$ MeV.
   209‚Üí
   210‚ÜíWe use natural units $c = \hbar = 1$.  The Koide ratio is denoted $Q$.  We write
   211‚Üí$\mathfrak{z}_k = \pm\sqrt{m_k}$ for signed mass charges, with the sign conventions
   212‚Üíspecified for each triple.  Pulls are quoted as
   213‚Üí$(x_{\text{pred}} - x_{\text{obs}})/\sigma_{\text{obs}}$ in units of $\sigma$.
   214‚Üí
   215‚Üí### 1.6 Outline
   216‚Üí
   217‚ÜíSection 2 reviews the Koide parametrization and the seed structure.
   218‚ÜíSection 3 presents the quark chain: the seed, the signed triple, and the heavy triple.
   219‚ÜíSection 4 discusses the $v_0$-doubling and the overlap prediction.
   220‚ÜíSection 5 extends the analysis to pseudoscalar mesons.
   221‚ÜíSection 6 covers the phase relation $\delta_0 \bmod 2\pi/3 \approx 2/9$ and the dual
   222‚ÜíKoide.
   223‚ÜíSection 7 presents the de Vries angle and the GST relation.
   224‚ÜíSection 8 gives the full statistical assessment, including combined significance.
   225‚ÜíSection 9 discusses scheme dependence, renormalization stability, and negative results.
   226‚ÜíSection 10 summarizes the conclusions and the connection to [I].
   227‚Üí
   228‚Üí---
   229‚Üí
   230‚Üí### References (partial, to be completed)
   231‚Üí
   232‚Üí[1] Y. Koide, Phys. Rev. Lett. 47, 1241 (1981).
   233‚Üí
   234‚Üí[2] Particle Data Group, R.L. Workman et al., Prog. Theor. Exp. Phys. 2022, 083C01.
   235‚Üí
   236‚Üí[3] R. Foot, arXiv:hep-ph/9402242.
   237‚Üí
   238‚Üí[4] D. Look and M.D. Sheppeard (see [13] and references therein).
   239‚Üí
   240‚Üí[5] Y. Koide, Mod. Phys. Lett. A 5, 2319 (1990).
   241‚Üí
   242‚Üí[6] E. Ma, Phys. Rev. D 73, 077301 (2006).
   243‚Üí
   244‚Üí[7] Z.-z. Xing, H. Zhang, and S. Zhou, Phys. Lett. B 666, 462 (2008).
   245‚Üí
   246‚Üí[8] M. Ratz, Lecture notes (2013).
   247‚Üí
   248‚Üí[9] F. Wilczek, Nature 439, 547 (2006).
   249‚Üí
   250‚Üí[10] Computed in this work; Monte Carlo with $10^4$ trials, see Section 8.
   251‚Üí
   252‚Üí[11] S. Esposito and P. Santorelli, Mod. Phys. Lett. A 10, 3077 (1995).
   253‚Üí
   254‚Üí[12] W. Rodejohann and H. Zhang, Phys. Lett. B 698, 152 (2011).
   255‚Üí
   256‚Üí[13] A. Rivero, arXiv:1111.7232 [hep-ph].
   257‚Üí
   258‚Üí[14] A. Rivero, arXiv:0710.1526 [hep-ph].
   259‚Üí
   260‚Üí[15] P. Zenczykowski, Phys. Rev. D 86, 117303 (2012).
   261‚Üí

   263‚Üí

The draft is complete. Here is a summary of what was written and where it lives.

---

**File**: `/home/codexssh/phys3/results/paper_b_intro.md`

**Structure**:

**Abstract** (~230 words): Compiles all ten Koide-family observations with their numerical values and statistical significances, plus the de Vries angle and GST relation. States the paper's thesis concisely: individually modest, collectively they point to a common algebraic structure. References the companion paper [I] for the Lagrangian interpretation.

**Introduction** (~2 pages, six subsections):

- **1.1 The Koide formula**: States the formula, gives the current numerical value $Q = 0.666661 \pm 0.000007$, the $m_\tau$ prediction, and Foot's geometric reformulation. Notes four decades of theoretical attempts without consensus.

- **1.2 The skeptic's objection**: Addresses look-elsewhere ($2.8\sigma$ from Monte Carlo over all 84 triples with 8 sign assignments) and renormalization scheme dependence (the formula holds better at low energies, which is where boundary conditions from an effective superpotential would apply, by analogy with Gell-Mann--Okubo and GST).

- **1.3 Beyond the leptons**: The core of the introduction. Lists all ten observations with references, numerical values, deviations, and significance assessments. Includes the two non-Koide relations (de Vries angle, GST). Every number matches the audited values from the project's `numerical_audit.md`.

- **1.4 What this paper adds**: Three contributions: (1) a self-contained compilation with error bars, (2) rigorous look-elsewhere analysis defining trial spaces explicitly, (3) the argument that the observations are correlated (overlapping masses, obligatory up/down mixing, common phase). Previews the O'Raifeartaigh result from [I] with the single constraint $gv/m = \sqrt{3}$.

- **1.5 Conventions and mass inputs**: PDG values, mass scheme conventions, notation for signed mass charges, pull convention.

- **1.6 Outline**: Ten-section plan covering the full paper.

**References**: 19 entries, partial, covering Koide (1981), PDG, Foot, Xing et al., Rodejohann-Zhang, the author's prior papers, Zenczykowski, Sheppeard, de Vries, and Gatto-Sartori-Tonin/Oakes.

All numerical claims in the draft are consistent with the audited values in `results/numerical_audit.md`. The meson triple deviation is stated as $0.10\%$ (consistent with the computed $0.104\%$). The $v_0$-doubling look-elsewhere probability of $24.9\%$ is honestly reported as not significant in isolation.
